actions.label: Risk assessment
actions.text:
MH risk assessment:
- Current intent / plan / means:
- Recent triggers:
- Protective factors:
- Past attempts / hospital admissions:
- Substance use:
- MH services involvement:
- Capacity:

Action:
- Same-day F2F vs phone vs crisis team
- Safety plan documented
- Safeguarding referral if children in household
- Follow-up timeframe agreed

actions.label: Safeguarding check
actions.text:
Always consider:
• Children / dependents in household → safeguarding referral threshold
• Mental Capacity Assessment
• Document risk assessment in full
• Crisis team contact readily available
• Clear follow-up plan with named clinician

actions.label: Documentation
actions.text:
Safeguarding considerations:
• Notify practice safeguarding lead
• MARAC / DASH risk assessment if DV suspected
• Code in record (Safeguarding adult / child concern)
• Document concerns verbatim
• Information sharing per local policy
• Children in household — separate referral if at risk

rules.label: Chest pain
patterns: pain in my jaw and chest
pain in my left arm and sweat
pain in my left arm with sweat
pain in my left arm and nausea
pain in my left arm with nausea
pain in my right arm and sweat
pain in my right arm with sweat
pain in my left shoulder and sweat
pain in my left shoulder with sweat

actions.label: Acute pathway
actions.text:
If acute / ongoing pain → 999.

If resolved:
• Onset, duration, character, radiation, exertion-related?
• Cardiac risk factors: age, smoker, FH, DM, lipids, BP, prior CVD
• Associated: SOB, sweating, N&V, syncope
• Differential: ACS, PE, dissection, pericarditis, GORD, MSK, anxiety

Thresholds:
• HEART score for risk stratification
• Same-day clinical assessment if any concerning features

actions.label: HEART score template
actions.text:
HEART score:
- History (suspicious 0/1/2):
- ECG (normal / non-spec / abnormal 0/1/2):
- Age (<45 / 45-64 / >65 = 0/1/2):
- Risk factors (count: 0 / 1-2 / 3+ or known CVD = 0/1/2):
- Troponin (norm / 1-3x / >3x ULN = 0/1/2):

Total: __
0-3 = low risk · 4-6 = moderate · 7-10 = high

rules.label: Cauda equina
patterns: can.?t feel (when I |to |myself )?(wee|urinate|pee|pass urine|empty my bladder)
numb (bum|bottom|groin|genitals|perineum|between my legs|between the legs|down below|in my groin|around my back passage|around my anus|around my back\w*)
numbness (in my )?(bum|bottom|groin|genitals|perineum|between (my |the )?legs|down below|around my back passage|saddle area)
loss of sensation (in my )?(bum|bottom|groin|genitals|perineum|between (my |the )?legs|saddle area|down below)
can.?t feel (myself |when I )?(pee|wee|go to the toilet)

actions.label: Emergency MRI
actions.text:
Suspected cauda equina = same-day emergency MRI.

Red flags (any):
• Bilateral sciatica
• Saddle anaesthesia / paraesthesia
• Bladder dysfunction (retention / overflow)
• Bowel incontinence / loss of anal tone
• Sexual dysfunction

Action:
• Discuss with on-call neurosurgery / spinal team
• Direct ED referral if no urgent MRI access
• Document neuro exam including PR if appropriate

rules.label: Thunderclap headache
patterns: headache came on out of nowhere
worst pain of my life in my head
head like it was going to explode
worst pain ever in my head
sudden severe headache and stiff neck
sudden severe headache with stiff neck

actions.label: SAH pathway
actions.text:
Thunderclap headache → consider SAH.

• Maximum intensity within seconds-minutes
• Same-day CT head (CT >95% sensitive within 6h)
• If CT negative & onset <6h, may exclude
• If onset >6h or symptoms ongoing, LP at 12h

Other differentials: cervical artery dissection, RCVS, pituitary apoplexy, venous sinus thrombosis.

Action: same-day ED referral.

actions.label: Acute pathway
actions.text:
Suspected upper / lower GI bleed.

• Haemodynamics: HR, BP, lying/standing
• Anticoagulants / antiplatelets / NSAIDs
• Liver disease / known varices
• Glasgow-Blatchford for upper GI bleed

Action:
• Acute / unstable → 999
• Stable + suspicious → same-day medical admission
• Painless rectal bleeding > 50y → 2WW colorectal

actions.label: Glasgow-Blatchford template
actions.text:
Glasgow-Blatchford:
- Urea (mmol/L):
- Hb (g/L):
- Systolic BP:
- Pulse ≥100:
- Melaena:
- Syncope:
- Hepatic disease:
- Cardiac failure:

Score: __
0 = consider outpatient · ≥1 = admit

actions.label: DMARD monitoring
actions.text:
Methotrexate monitoring (NICE / BSR):

• FBC + U&E + LFT every 12 weeks (or 4-weekly if dose change / new abnormalities)
• Annual chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms
• Pneumococcal + annual flu vaccine
• Folic acid 5mg weekly (≥24h after MTX)

Warnings:
• Teratogenic — 6mo washout F, 3mo M before conception
• Hepatotoxic — alcohol limits
• Pulmonary toxicity — investigate any new SOB / dry cough
• Drug interactions — esp. trimethoprim, NSAIDs (caution)

actions.label: Bloods reminder
actions.text:
Methotrexate monitoring bloods:
- FBC + film:
- U&E:
- LFTs:
- Date of last MTX dose:
- Current dose:
- Folic acid:
- Symptoms? (SOB, mouth ulcers, infection):

Next review due:

actions.label: Lithium monitoring
actions.text:
Lithium monitoring (NICE):

• Level 12h post-dose, 3-monthly (more often if dose change, illness, drug interaction)
• U&E + TFT + Ca every 6 months
• Target range typically 0.6-0.8 mmol/L (0.4-1.0 acceptable)
• Toxicity above 1.5 mmol/L

Common traps:
• Dehydration / D&V → toxicity
• NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides ↑ levels
• Pregnancy considerations — specialist advice
• Hypothyroidism long-term

actions.label: Anticoag review
actions.text:
Annual anticoagulant review:

• Indication still valid?
• Renal function — dose adjustment for DOACs
• Bleeding risk reassessment (HAS-BLED, ORBIT)
• CHA₂DS₂-VASc for AF (consider stopping if low risk)
• Patient knows what to do if a dose is missed
• Has Yellow Book / DOAC alert card
• Interacting meds review (esp. NSAIDs, antibiotics, antifungals)
• Bleeding episodes — frequency, severity, source
• Falls risk if elderly

rules.label: UTI
patterns: nothing comes? out (when I try to )?(wee|pee)

actions.label: UTI history & exam
actions.text:
UTI history:
- Dysuria / frequency / urgency:
- Suprapubic pain / loin pain:
- Duration of symptoms:
- Fever / rigors / vomiting:
- Vaginal discharge:
- Sexually active / new partner:
- Pregnancy possible:
- Catheter / structural abnormality:
- Recent abx / hospitalisation:
- Recurrence (≥3 in 12mo or ≥2 in 6mo):
- Diabetes / immunosuppression:

O/E:
- Vitals (sepsis screen):
- Abdominal exam:
- Loin tenderness:
- (Dipstick: not for ≥65y or catheterised):

Plan:
- 1st line: nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim per local sensitivity
- 2nd line: pivmecillinam / fosfomycin
- Pyelonephritis features → admit / IV abx

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (UTI)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — uncomplicated lower UTI, women aged 16–64, not pregnant. If eligible, signpost to community pharmacy.

Not suitable / safety-net: pregnant, male, <16 or >64, recurrent UTI, catheter, immunosuppressed, suspected pyelonephritis (fever, loin/back pain, rigors, vomiting), visible haematuria, or not improving in 48h.

actions.label: Airway emergency
actions.text:
Suspected adult epiglottitis / supraglottitis → 999 / same-day ED.

Red flags (any): drooling or unable to swallow saliva, muffled “hot potato” voice, stridor, sitting forward to breathe, severe sore throat with a normal-looking oropharynx, rapid deterioration.

Action:
• Do NOT attempt to examine the throat or use a tongue depressor — risk of precipitating airway obstruction
• Keep the patient sitting upright; do not lie flat
• 999 transfer / immediate ENT-anaesthetics involvement
• Differential: quinsy, deep neck-space infection, anaphylaxis

actions.label: FeverPAIN scoring
actions.text:
FeverPAIN score (1 point each):
- Fever in last 24h:
- Purulence (tonsils):
- Attended within 3 days of onset:
- Inflamed tonsils (severe):
- No cough or coryza:

Total: __
0-1: 13-18% strep — no abx, self-care
2-3: 34-40% strep — delayed abx (3d) or no abx
4-5: 62-65% strep — immediate abx

Abx of choice: phenoxymethylpenicillin 500mg QDS 5-10d (clarithromycin if pen-allergic)

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (sore throat)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — acute sore throat, age 5+. Pharmacist applies FeverPAIN.

Safety-net / urgent: difficulty breathing, difficulty swallowing saliva, drooling, muffled “hot potato” voice, stridor, neck stiffness, systemically very unwell, or immunosuppressed → same-day assessment (?quinsy / epiglottitis).

actions.label: OM history & exam
actions.text:
Otitis media history:
- Onset / duration:
- Fever / unwell:
- Otalgia laterality:
- Discharge / hearing loss:
- Recent URTI:
- Recurrent OM (≥3 in 6mo or ≥4/yr):
- Eardrum perforation history:
- Grommets:

O/E:
- TM appearance: bulging / erythematous / dull / perforated / effusion
- Mastoid tenderness:
- General — temp, fluid intake:

Plan:
- Most resolve in 3 days, paracetamol / ibuprofen
- Abx if: <2y bilateral, perforation + discharge, systemically unwell, deteriorating
- Amoxicillin 5-7d (clarithromycin if pen-allergic)
- Safety net: persistent fever 3-4d, mastoid swelling, hearing loss >2-4w

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (otitis media)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — acute otitis media, age 1–17. If eligible, signpost to community pharmacy.

Not suitable / safety-net: <1y or >17y, systemically very unwell, immunosuppressed, suspected mastoiditis (swelling/redness behind the ear), or symptoms not improving after 3 days.

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (sinusitis)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — acute sinusitis, age 12+. If eligible, signpost to community pharmacy.

Safety-net / urgent: symptoms >10 days, swelling or redness around the eye, double vision, severe frontal headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, or systemically very unwell.

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (infected insect bite)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — infected insect bite, age 1+. If eligible, signpost to community pharmacy.

Safety-net / urgent: rapidly spreading redness, fever or systemic illness, suspected cellulitis, bite near the eye, or breathing difficulty / facial swelling suggesting anaphylaxis → emergency.

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (impetigo)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — impetigo, age 1+ (non-bullous, localised). If eligible, signpost to community pharmacy.

Not suitable / refer: bullous impetigo, widespread or recurrent, systemically unwell, immunosuppressed, or not improving with treatment.

rules.label: Shingles
patterns: burning pain (on |down )?(one side|the left|the right)( of my (chest|back|body|face))?

actions.label: Pharmacy First referral (shingles)
actions.text:
Consider Pharmacy First — shingles, age 18+. Antiviral most effective within 72h of rash onset.

Urgent / not suitable for PF: eye involvement or rash on the tip of the nose (Hutchinson’s sign — ?ophthalmic zoster), immunocompromised, pregnant, severe / widespread / disseminated rash, or signs of complications.

actions.label: Back pain assessment
actions.text:
Back pain — red flags (refer urgently):
- Cauda equina (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel)
- Significant trauma
- Age <20 or >55 new onset
- Progressive neurological deficit
- Fever / weight loss / night pain (cancer/infection)
- IVDU / immunosuppression
- History of malignancy
- Severe progressive limb weakness

Yellow flags (psychosocial):
- Belief pain is harmful
- Avoidance behaviour
- Low mood / withdrawal
- Job dissatisfaction
- Compensation/legal issues

Management (no red flags):
- Stay active, return to normal activities
- Analgesia: NSAIDs first-line (with PPI cover if indicated)
- Avoid opioids and gabapentinoids for non-specific LBP
- Refer to physio / MSK if not improving in 4-6 weeks

actions.label: Resp red flags
actions.text:
Cough / breathlessness — concerning features:
• Haemoptysis
• Weight loss / night sweats
• Smoker / ex-smoker, esp. age >40
• Persistent cough >3 weeks
• Hoarseness >3 weeks
• Acute onset breathlessness (PE / pneumothorax)
• Tachypnoea, hypoxia, accessory muscle use
• Single-lobe / focal signs
• Failure to respond to abx

Thresholds:
• Same-day F2F if any red flag, hypoxia, or systemic unwellness
• 2WW chest if haemoptysis + age >40, or persistent cough + smoker

actions.label: Resp assessment
actions.text:
Respiratory presentation:
- Onset / duration:
- Cough: dry / productive (colour, consistency):
- Haemoptysis:
- Breathlessness — exertional / rest, MRC grade:
- Wheeze:
- Fever, night sweats, weight loss:
- Chest pain — pleuritic / cardiac:
- PE risk: travel, immobility, surgery, OCP, malignancy, leg swelling:
- Smoking pack-years:
- Asthma / COPD:

O/E:
- Vitals: HR, BP, RR, sats, temp:
- WOB / accessory muscles:
- Chest exam:
- PEFR (if asthma):

Plan:

actions.label: Asthma control (RCP3Q)
actions.text:
RCP 3 Questions (any “yes” = poor control → review):
1. Recent sleep disturbance from asthma?
2. Usual daytime symptoms (cough/wheeze/tight chest)?
3. Interference with usual activity?

Reliever use ≥3×/week or any night waking = uncontrolled → inhaler technique + adherence + step-up review.

ACUTE SEVERE (same-day): PEF 33–50% best, can’t complete sentences, RR ≥25, HR ≥110.
LIFE-THREATENING (999): PEF <33%, silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, SpO₂ <92%.
After any attack: steroid course completed? Follow-up within 48h.

actions.label: 7-point checklist
actions.text:
Weighted 7-point checklist for pigmented lesion (refer 2WW if score ≥3 OR any major):

Major (2 points each):
- Change in size:
- Irregular shape:
- Irregular colour:

Minor (1 point each):
- Largest diameter ≥7mm:
- Inflammation:
- Oozing / bleeding:
- Change in sensation (itch):

Total: __

Lesion description:
- Site:
- Diameter:
- Colour:
- Border:
- Evolution (timeline):
- Symptoms:
- ABCDE: Asymmetry / Border / Colour / Diameter / Evolving:

Photo on record? Y/N

actions.label: MH consultation
actions.text:
MH presentation:
- Onset / duration / triggers:
- Symptoms (mood, anhedonia, sleep, appetite, energy, concentration):
- Anxiety symptoms / panic:
- Functional impact (work, relationships, self-care):
- Risk: thoughts of self-harm / suicide / harm to others:
- Substance use:
- Past MH history / family history:
- Social context: housing, finances, support network:
- Recent losses / life events:
- PHQ-9: __ / GAD-7: __

Plan:
- Self-help / IAPT / counselling
- SSRI if moderate-severe (caution under 25 — review 2 weekly):
- Safety net + follow-up:

actions.label: Post-discharge checklist
actions.text:
After hospital discharge — review:

• Medication reconciliation (new / changed / stopped)
• Follow-up appointments booked?
• Outstanding investigations / results pending?
• Self-monitoring / safety-net advice given?
• District nurse / community support arranged?
• Social: home situation, carer, equipment
• Code recent admission in record
• Update problem list and registers (e.g. HF, COPD, AF post-MI)
• Consider proactive care plan if frail / multimorbid

actions.label: Post-discharge review
actions.text:
Post-discharge review:

Admission:
- Dates:
- Diagnoses:
- Procedures:
- Discharge medications (new/changed):
- Follow-up planned:

Review today:
- Recovery progress:
- Symptoms — concerning / improving:
- Pain control:
- Mobility / ADLs:
- Med compliance / side effects:
- Outstanding actions chased:
- Safety net advice:

actions.label: SMR template
actions.text:
Structured Medication Review (SMR):

Patient understanding:
- Knows why each med is prescribed:
- Adherence / compliance:
- Side effects experienced:
- Self-monitoring (BP, BG, peak flow):

For each medication:
- Indication still valid?
- Dose appropriate (renal/hepatic adjustment)?
- Duplications / interactions / cascades?
- Anticholinergic burden / falls risk?
- Recent monitoring done?

Deprescribe candidates:
- PPI > 8 weeks without indication
- Long-term benzo / Z-drug
- Statin in extreme frailty / limited life expectancy
- Anti-hypertensives if postural hypotension
- Aspirin without clear secondary prevention indication

Agreed plan:

actions.label: Fit note template
actions.text:
Med3 considerations:

- Diagnosis (the condition affecting work):
- Functional limitations (what can / can't do):
- Duration: <3mo (any duration) / >3mo (max 3mo):
- 'Not fit' or 'May be fit' (consider phased return / amended duties / altered hours / workplace adaptations):
- Assessment date and end date:
- Comments / advice to employer:

For mental health:
- Avoid jargon — concrete functional language
- Consider OH referral / IAPT signposting

For MSK:
- Active management plan
- Avoid bed rest > 1-2 days

actions.label: HRT consultation
actions.text:
Menopause / HRT consultation:

Symptoms:
- Vasomotor (flushes, sweats, night sweats):
- Mood, sleep, cognition:
- GU: vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary:
- Joint / muscle pain:
- Libido:
- Functional impact:

History:
- LMP / cycle changes:
- Contraception still needed?
- Migraine with aura?
- VTE / clotting history (personal / family):
- Breast / endometrial / ovarian cancer history:
- BMI / smoking / alcohol:
- Cardiovascular risk:
- Liver disease:
- Current meds:

Discussion:
- Risk / benefit balance
- Options: sequential vs continuous combined; oestrogen-only if hysterectomy; transdermal preferred if VTE / migraine / hepatic / >60
- Vaginal oestrogen for GU symptoms (no extra systemic risk)
- Testosterone for low libido (off-label)
- Lifestyle, CBT for vasomotor

Plan / review timeframe:

actions.label: FAST pathway
actions.text:
Suspected stroke (FAST: Face droop / Arm weakness / Speech / Time) with symptoms NOW or within hours → 999. Thrombolysis & thrombectomy are time-critical.

Resolved deficit (?TIA): high early stroke risk — aspirin 300mg (unless contraindicated) + specialist TIA-clinic assessment within 24h (NICE NG128). Do NOT “wait and see”.

Mimics (hypo, migraine aura, Bell’s palsy) — treat as stroke until assessed.

actions.label: Sepsis screen
actions.text:
Could this be sepsis? (NICE NG51 risk stratification.)

High-risk features → 999 / immediate F2F:
• New altered mental state / hard to rouse
• Mottled, ashen or cyanosed skin; non-blanching rash
• RR ≥25, new O₂ requirement
• HR ≥130, SBP ≤90
• Not passed urine in 18h
• Rigors / uncontrollable shaking with fever

Remember NEUTROPENIC sepsis: chemotherapy in last 6 weeks + fever = emergency admission, no delays.

actions.label: Anaphylaxis pathway
actions.text:
Airway/breathing/circulation involvement with an allergic trigger = anaphylaxis.

• 999 immediately
• IM adrenaline 1:1000, 500 micrograms (adult) anterolateral thigh; repeat after 5 min if no improvement
• Lie flat, raise legs (sit up only if breathing difficulty); do NOT stand the patient up
• After emergency treatment: ensure 2 adrenaline auto-injectors carried + specialist allergy-clinic referral

Rash/itch alone without airway, breathing or circulation features is not anaphylaxis — antihistamine + safety-net.

actions.label: Meningitis / meningococcal
actions.text:
Fever + any of: non-blanching/petechial rash, neck stiffness, photophobia, new confusion or drowsiness → 999 (NICE NG240, 2024).

• Non-blanching rash + fever = meningococcal sepsis until proven otherwise (may occur WITHOUT meningitis)
• Give IM/IV benzylpenicillin (or ceftriaxone) pre-hospital ONLY if transfer will be delayed — never delay transfer to give it
• Glass/tumbler test is the patient-facing check for blanching

actions.label: Vascular emergency
actions.text:
Sudden tearing/ripping back, abdominal or chest pain ± collapse, pallor, sweating, or a pulsatile abdominal mass = ruptured AAA / aortic dissection until proven otherwise.

• 999 — do not bring to surgery for assessment
• Highest suspicion: older men, smokers, hypertension, known aneurysm/FH
• Mimics renal colic in the elderly — first presentation of “renal colic” over 60 should raise AAA

actions.label: Surgical emergency
actions.text:
Sudden severe unilateral testicular pain ± nausea/vomiting = torsion until proven otherwise — the salvage window is ~6 hours.

• Same-day urology / ED NOW — do not arrange routine review or wait for swelling to settle
• Most common 12–25y but any age
• Do not rely on ultrasound availability to decide — clinical suspicion is enough for referral

actions.label: VTE / limb-ischaemia pathway
actions.text:
Suspected PE: acute breathlessness ± pleuritic pain ± haemoptysis. Haemodynamic compromise or hypoxia → 999; otherwise same-day assessment (Wells → D-dimer / CTPA) per NICE NG158.

Suspected DVT: unilateral leg swelling/heat/tenderness → same-day Wells scoring + proximal leg ultrasound pathway. Do not massage the leg.

COLD, PALE, PULSELESS limb (6 P’s) = acute limb ischaemia → 999 / vascular surgery — hours matter.

actions.label: Wells score (DVT)
actions.text:
Wells DVT (1 point each unless stated):
- Active cancer:
- Paralysis / recent plaster immobilisation:
- Bed >3d or major surgery <12wk:
- Tenderness along deep venous system:
- Entire leg swollen:
- Calf >3cm larger than other side:
- Pitting oedema (symptomatic leg):
- Collateral superficial veins:
- Previous DVT:
- Alternative diagnosis at least as likely: -2

≥2 DVT likely → USS within 4h (or anticoagulate + USS within 24h)
≤1 DVT unlikely → D-dimer

actions.label: Surgical abdomen
actions.text:
Peritonitic features (rigid/board-like abdomen, rebound, guarding, pain on movement/coughing, lying still) → same-day surgical assessment / 999 if shocked.

Consider: perforation, pancreatitis, ischaemic bowel (pain out of proportion), appendicitis, ruptured ectopic.

Women of reproductive age with acute lower abdominal pain: ALWAYS pregnancy test (?ectopic).

actions.label: NICE traffic light
actions.text:
Temperature ≥38°C in an infant UNDER 3 MONTHS is a RED traffic-light feature (NICE NG143) → same-day paediatric assessment (usually ED), regardless of how well the baby looks.

3–6 months ≥39°C = amber minimum.

Any age + fever with: drowsy/floppy, poor feeding, non-blanching rash, grunting/recession, reduced wet nappies → escalate same-day.

actions.label: Paediatric respiratory distress
actions.text:
Signs of increased work of breathing in a child — recession (ribs/tummy sucking in), grunting, nasal flaring, stridor at rest, cyanosis/blue lips, too breathless to feed or talk → same-day assessment; 999 if stridor at rest, cyanosis, exhaustion or drowsiness.

Croup: barky cough + stridor — oral dexamethasone if more than mild.
Bronchiolitis (<2y): escalate if feeding <50% of normal, apnoeas, marked recession, sats concern.

actions.label: Seizure pathway
actions.text:
• Seizure ongoing >5 min, repeated seizures, or not recovering consciousness → 999 (status epilepticus)
• FIRST-ever seizure (any age) → same-day assessment + urgent first-fit clinic referral; advise not to drive (DVLA)
• Febrile convulsion (6m–5y): usually benign if simple (<15 min, generalised, full recovery) but first episode warrants same-day review and exclusion of meningitis/serious infection
• Known epilepsy with typical self-terminated seizure → prompt review, check triggers/adherence

actions.label: Early pregnancy pathway
actions.text:
Pain and/or bleeding in (possible) pregnancy → same-day Early Pregnancy Unit assessment.

• Ectopic can present with PAIN BEFORE any bleeding — one-sided pain, shoulder-tip pain, dizziness/collapse in early pregnancy = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise → 999
• Always pregnancy-test women of reproductive age with abdominal pain
• Anti-D consideration for surgical management of miscarriage

rules.label: Reduced fetal movements
patterns: baby (not|isn.?t|stopped) (moving|kicking)( as much| much| at all)?
baby (is )?(quieter|less active|still|not as active)( than usual)?

actions.label: RFM pathway (RCOG GTG57)
actions.text:
Any maternal perception of reduced fetal movements (after ~24 weeks) → signpost DIRECTLY to maternity triage / day assessment unit NOW for CTG ± ultrasound.

• Do NOT book a GP appointment or advise “wait and count kicks”
• Never reassure by phone without fetal assessment
• Repeat episodes still warrant assessment every time

actions.label: Pre-eclampsia pathway
actions.text:
Pregnancy (especially >20 weeks) + severe/persistent headache, visual disturbance, epigastric/RUQ pain, or sudden swelling of face/hands → same-day maternity assessment for BP + urine protein.

• Can progress rapidly to eclampsia/HELLP
• Seizure, severe hypertension or reduced consciousness → 999
• Direct to maternity triage, not a routine GP slot

actions.label: Sight-threatening pathway
actions.text:
• Sudden painless loss of vision in one eye → same-day emergency ophthalmology (?CRAO/CRVO/retinal detachment/vitreous haemorrhage)
• Curtain/shadow over vision, or flashes + NEW floaters → same-day (?retinal detachment/tear)
• GCA: age >50 + new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication ± visual symptoms → SAME-DAY high-dose steroid + urgent ophthalmology/rheumatology + ESR/CRP — the second eye is at risk
• Painful red eye + visual loss → ?acute angle-closure → emergency

actions.label: Septic arthritis pathway
actions.text:
An acutely hot, swollen, very painful joint (especially single joint ± fever) = septic arthritis until proven otherwise.

• Same-day orthopaedics / ED for joint aspiration BEFORE antibiotics
• An untreated septic joint is destroyed within days
• Prosthetic joint, immunosuppression, recent injection = even lower threshold
• Do not assume gout if febrile or systemically unwell

actions.label: First-episode psychosis
actions.text:
Hallucinations, delusions, thought disorder or marked paranoia → urgent referral to Early Intervention in Psychosis (EIP) — NICE: assessment started within 2 weeks; same-day crisis-team input if risk.

• Command hallucinations / risk to self or others → crisis pathway now
• Exclude organic causes (delirium, substances) — acute confusion in the elderly is delirium, not psychosis
• POSTPARTUM PSYCHOSIS = psychiatric emergency → same-day perinatal MH / crisis team

actions.label: 2WW pathway
actions.text:
NICE NG12: visible haematuria aged ≥45 (unexplained, or persisting/recurring after UTI treatment) → 2WW urology. Aged ≥60 with unexplained non-visible haematuria + dysuria or raised WCC → 2WW.

Under the age cutoffs: still assess (UTI, stones, anticoagulants) and safety-net — haematuria is never normal.

Exclude/treat UTI but do not let an infection label close the loop without confirming resolution.

actions.label: 2WW pathway
actions.text:
NICE NG12: post-menopausal bleeding (≥12 months after last period) in women ≥55 → 2WW gynaecology (transvaginal USS endometrial assessment); strongly consider for younger post-menopausal women too.

• Includes spotting, staining and blood-streaked discharge
• HRT breakthrough bleeding still needs assessment if unscheduled/persistent

actions.label: 2WW pathway
actions.text:
NICE NG12 breast referral:
• Aged ≥30 with an unexplained breast lump → 2WW
• Aged ≥50 with nipple discharge, retraction or other concerning unilateral nipple change → 2WW
• Skin changes suggestive of cancer (dimpling, peau d’orange, nipple eczema) or axillary lump → refer
• Under 30 with a lump: non-urgent referral/assessment, lower threshold if FH

If the story is feeding-related (mastitis), treat — but a lump persisting after mastitis resolves needs 2WW.

rules.label: Swallowing difficulty
patterns: something stuck (in my throat|when i swallow)

actions.label: 2WW pathway
actions.text:
NICE NG12:
• Dysphagia (food sticking, progressive difficulty swallowing) at ANY age → 2WW upper-GI endoscopy
• Persistent hoarseness >3 weeks (especially >45y or smoker) → 2WW ENT + consider CXR

Acute painful swallow with sore throat/tonsillitis is different — see sore-throat rule. Difficulty swallowing SALIVA / drooling → same-day (?epiglottitis/quinsy).

actions.label: Diabetic emergency
actions.text:
Suspected DKA or HHS → same-day / 999.

• DKA (type 1): vomiting, abdominal pain, raised ketones, deep/fast (Kussmaul) breathing, fruity breath, drowsiness
• HHS (elderly type 2): very high glucose + marked dehydration + confusion, usually without significant ketones

Action:
• Check capillary glucose AND ketones now if able
• Capillary ketones ≥3.0 mmol/L (or urine ketones ++), or vomiting and unable to keep fluids down → emergency admission
• NEVER omit insulin - arrange immediate transfer

rules.label: Diabetes problem
patterns: blood sugars? (too high|very high|sky high|dangerously high|too low|very low|in the \d+s)
blood sugars? (won.?t come down|keep dropping|all over the place)

actions.label: Escalation thresholds
actions.text:
RED FLAGS → same-day / 999:
• ?DKA (esp. type 1): vomiting, abdominal pain, ketones, deep/fast breathing, fruity breath, drowsiness
• ?HHS (esp. elderly type 2): very high glucose + dehydration + confusion
• Severe hypo (needed help / unconscious / seizure) → 999; IM glucagon if available
• Vomiting and cannot keep fluids down → same-day

Sick-day rules: NEVER stop insulin; check glucose & ketones 2–4-hourly; keep drinking; ketones rising or persistent vomiting → urgent review.

Routine “sugars running high” without red flags → prompt clinical review/titration, not emergency.

actions.label: Dehydration assessment
actions.text:
Concerning: ≥50% fewer wet nappies, no tears, dry mouth, sunken eyes/fontanelle, lethargy, not tolerating fluids → same-day assessment.

RED (→ 999/ED): mottled, cold extremities, cap refill >2s, very drowsy/floppy, no urine >12–24h, persistent bilious vomiting.

Mild D&V tolerating ORS → small frequent fluids + strict safety-net. Bulging fontanelle = different emergency (?meningitis).

actions.label: Infant vomiting red flags
actions.text:
• GREEN/BILIOUS vomiting in an infant = intestinal obstruction (?malrotation/volvulus) until proven otherwise → SAME-DAY surgical/ED — this is an emergency
• Projectile vomiting in a 2–8-week-old, hungry after, weight faltering → ?pyloric stenosis → same-day referral
• Vomiting + drowsy/floppy/bulging fontanelle → ?meningitis/raised ICP → emergency
• Effortless small possets in a well, gaining baby = reflux — reassure + safety-net

rules.label: Head injury
patterns: won.?t (wake up|stay awake) after (hitting|banging|the bang on) (his |her |their )?head

actions.label: NG232 CT criteria
actions.text:
CT head WITHIN 1 HOUR (→ ED now) if any: GCS <13 initially or <15 at 2h; suspected open/depressed or basal skull fracture (CSF leak, panda eyes, Battle’s sign); post-traumatic seizure; focal neurology; >1 episode of vomiting.

ON ANTICOAGULANT (warfarin/DOAC) or with bleeding disorder: CT within 8h after ANY head injury, even if entirely well — send to ED.

LOC or amnesia + (age ≥65 / dangerous mechanism / clotting issue) → CT within 8h.

Children: lower threshold; <1y with any bruise/swelling on head → assess; consider NAI if mechanism inconsistent.

actions.label: Limping child pathway
actions.text:
Limp + fever or systemically unwell = septic arthritis/osteomyelitis until proven otherwise → same-day paediatric/ortho assessment (bloods + imaging).

• Non-weight-bearing at any age → same-day (fracture, septic hip)
• Child <3y with a limp → same-day assessment (and consider NAI)
• Adolescent with hip/knee pain + limp → ?SUFE → urgent
• Transient synovitis is a diagnosis of exclusion

rules.label: Newborn jaundice
patterns: jaundice (within|in the first) (24 hours|first day|day one)

actions.label: Neonatal jaundice (NG98)
actions.text:
• Jaundice in the FIRST 24 HOURS of life = always pathological → same-day urgent serum bilirubin + paediatric assessment
• Visible jaundice any time in first 2 weeks → measure bilirubin (transcutaneous/serum) and plot on treatment-threshold chart — do not estimate by eye
• Jaundice + poor feeding/drowsy → urgent same-day
• Prolonged jaundice (>14 days term, >21 days preterm) → prolonged-jaundice screen incl. CONJUGATED bilirubin (?biliary atresia — pale stools/dark urine)

actions.label: Adult jaundice pathway
actions.text:
New jaundice in an adult → same-day bloods (LFTs, FBC, U&E, INR) and urgent assessment.

• PAINLESS jaundice (esp. >40y, weight loss, dark urine/pale stools) is a pancreatic-cancer red flag → NICE NG12 urgent CT/2WW pathway
• Jaundice + fever/RUQ pain → ?cholangitis → same-day admission
• Jaundice + confusion/bruising → ?acute liver failure → emergency
• Check paracetamol overdose, alcohol, new drugs (incl. herbal)

actions.label: 2WW pathway
actions.text:
NICE NG12: non-painful enlargement or change in shape/texture of the testis → 2WW urology (any age — peak 15–40y). Consider direct-access ultrasound if unsure whether mass is testicular.

SUDDEN SEVERE testicular PAIN = torsion → emergency (separate red rule).
Young men under-present — take written mentions seriously.

actions.label: VZV in pregnancy
actions.text:
Chickenpox CONTACT in pregnancy: same-day advice — check varicella history/immunity (IgG if uncertain); if non-immune and significant contact, post-exposure prophylaxis (antivirals/VZIG per current UKHSA guidance) is TIME-CRITICAL → discuss with obstetrics/virology today.

Chickenpox RASH in pregnancy: same-day clinical discussion — oral aciclovir if ≥20 weeks and within 24h of rash; severe disease/respiratory symptoms → admission. Risks: fetal varicella syndrome (<28wk), neonatal varicella (around delivery), maternal pneumonitis.

actions.label: EC time windows
actions.text:
Time-critical — deal with today:
• Copper IUD: most effective, up to 5 days (120h) after UPSI (or up to 5 days after earliest ovulation) — also ongoing contraception
• Ulipristal (ellaOne): up to 120h
• Levonorgestrel: up to 72h (less effective later; double dose if BMI/weight criteria per FSRH)

Community pharmacy can supply oral EC free via Pharmacy First. Safeguard: under-16s (Fraser), coercion/assault → follow safeguarding pathway. Offer STI screen + ongoing contraception; pregnancy test if next period late.

actions.label: Mastitis pathway
actions.text:
Lactational mastitis: continue feeding/expressing (milk drainage is the treatment), analgesia (paracetamol/ibuprofen safe), warm compress before feeds.

Antibiotics (flucloxacillin 10–14d; erythromycin if pen-allergic) if: systemically unwell, nipple fissure infection, or not improving after 12–24h of effective milk removal.

• Fluctuant mass → ?abscess → same-day referral for USS ± drainage
• A discrete lump or skin change persisting AFTER mastitis resolves → breast 2WW (inflammatory cancer mimics mastitis)
• Sepsis features → admit

rules.label: Heavy menstrual bleeding
patterns: bleeding (for|going on) (more than|over) (a week|7 days|two weeks)

actions.label: HMB pathway (NG88)
actions.text:
• Check FBC (± ferritin) — anaemia is common
• First-line: LNG-IUS; or tranexamic acid ± NSAID, or hormonal options
• Red flags out: intermenstrual/postcoital bleeding, post-menopausal bleeding (→ 2WW), pressure symptoms/mass (?fibroids → USS)
• Acute flooding + dizziness/syncope or Hb concern → same-day review
• Persistent IMB or treatment failure → gynae referral

actions.label: Postpartum red flags
actions.text:
Postpartum women deteriorate FAST — low threshold for same-day review/admission.

• Secondary PPH (heavy/increasing bleeding >24h–12wk post-delivery) → same-day; clots/offensive lochia → ?retained products/endometritis
• Fever + foul lochia + uterine tenderness = endometritis → same-day, often admission for IV antibiotics
• Wound infection: spreading erythema/systemic features → same-day
• Always consider sepsis (MBRRACE: genital-tract sepsis remains a leading cause of maternal death)

actions.label: Red eye red flags
actions.text:
Same-day ophthalmology if red eye PLUS any: pain (not just gritty), photophobia, reduced vision, haloes (?acute angle-closure), fixed/irregular pupil, CONTACT-LENS wearer (?microbial keratitis — lens out, same-day), herpetic vesicles, hypopyon, trauma.

Chemical injury: irrigate copiously NOW, then emergency referral.

Bland red eye, no pain/vision change = likely conjunctivitis → self-care/pharmacy + safety-net.

actions.label: SSNHL — urgent
actions.text:
Sudden (≤72h) unexplained hearing loss in one or both ears, not explained by wax/effusion on otoscopy, = sudden sensorineural hearing loss → URGENT same-day/24h ENT referral for audiometry + oral steroids — the treatment window is days, outcomes worsen with delay.

Do NOT routine-refer. Tuning fork (Weber lateralises AWAY from affected ear in SNHL) helps distinguish from conductive causes.

With vertigo/neurology → consider stroke pathway.

actions.label: Epistaxis pathway
actions.text:
First aid: sit forward, pinch the SOFT part of the nose firmly 10–15 min continuously, ice to bridge, spit don’t swallow.

• Not controlled after 2 × 15 min pressure → ED (cautery/packing)
• On anticoagulant/antiplatelet + heavy or recurrent bleeding → same-day review (check INR if warfarin) — low threshold for ED
• Posterior bleed (blood down throat both sides), haemodynamic symptoms → 999/ED
• Recurrent unilateral epistaxis + blocked nose in an adult → consider ENT referral (rare: tumour)

actions.label: Gout (NG219)
actions.text:
Acute flare: NSAID (with PPI) OR colchicine 500mcg bd–qds (cap dose; caution renal impairment/statins) OR short oral prednisolone — treat fast, any of the three.

• Do NOT stop allopurinol/febuxostat during a flare
• After the flare settles: offer urate-lowering therapy (treat-to-target serum urate <360 micromol/L; <300 if tophi/recurrent), start low + flare prophylaxis cover
• Hot swollen joint + fever/unwell → treat as SEPTIC arthritis, not gout
• Check U&E, consider CV risk/diuretic review

rules.label: Cellulitis?
patterns: (leg|arm) (is )?(red, hot and swollen|hot, red and swollen)

actions.label: Cellulitis pathway
actions.text:
Mark the border and date it. Uncomplicated: oral flucloxacillin (clarithromycin/doxycycline if pen-allergic), elevate, review 48h.

Admit / same-day ED if: systemic sepsis features, rapidly spreading, facial/periorbital (?orbital cellulitis — emergency), severe pain out of proportion (?necrotising — 999), immunosuppressed, failed orals.

Unilateral red swollen leg: consider DVT (often confused) and chronic venous eczema (usually bilateral, itchy, not febrile — doesn’t need antibiotics).

actions.label: ADR triage
actions.text:
Prescriber review needed — check timing vs drug start, dose, interactions (BNF).

SERIOUS — same-day/emergency:
• ACEi angioedema (lip/tongue swelling) → emergency
• New rash on allopurinol / lamotrigine / carbamazepine / penicillins → ?SJS/DRESS → stop + urgent review (mucosal involvement/blistering = emergency)
• Muscle pain + dark urine on statin → ?rhabdo → same-day CK
• Bleeding on anticoagulant → same-day

Report serious/unexpected reactions via MHRA Yellow Card.

actions.label: Delirium screen
actions.text:
ACUTE change in cognition/attention = delirium until proven otherwise — in an older person this is a same-day assessment (4AT screen).

Hunt the cause: infection (UTI/chest), urinary retention, constipation, dehydration, hypoxia, drugs (opioids, anticholinergics, benzos — recent changes), alcohol withdrawal, electrolytes/glucose, pain, stroke.

Fluctuating course + inattention distinguishes from dementia (chronic → memory-loss rule). Both can coexist — acute-on-chronic change still needs same-day review.

rules.label: Alcohol concern
patterns: drinking (every day|every morning|in the morning|first thing)

actions.label: Alcohol pathway
actions.text:
• Screen with AUDIT-C / full AUDIT
• DEPENDENT drinkers must NOT stop abruptly — withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens can kill; arrange planned/supported detox
• Already withdrawing (tremor, sweating, agitation, hallucinations, seizure) → same-day; severe → admission
• Oral thiamine (Wernicke prophylaxis); refer local community alcohol service
• Safeguarding: children/dependants at home; DVLA if relevant

actions.label: MEED risk assessment
actions.text:
Eating disorders kill — assess medical risk (RCPsych MEED), do not rely on appearance or BMI alone (bulimia/ARFID often normal weight).

Check: rate of weight loss, BMI/%mBMI, HR (bradycardia <50), postural BP drop, core temp, SUSS test (sit-up/squat-stand), ECG (QTc), U&E (K+!), phosphate, glucose.

• Physiological compromise → same-day medical admission (refeeding risk — admit, don’t “feed up at home”)
• Under-18 → urgent referral to community eating-disorder service/CAMHS-ED (NICE NG69: do not delay for weight thresholds)

actions.label: Perinatal MH pathway
actions.text:
• Ask directly about thoughts of self-harm and of harming the baby; intrusive “what if” thoughts WITH distress and no intent are common in perinatal OCD — distinguish from psychotic ideation
• Postpartum PSYCHOSIS (rapid onset days–weeks post-birth: confusion, mania, delusions about the baby) = psychiatric emergency → same-day perinatal/crisis team
• Refer perinatal mental-health team (priority access); EPDS/PHQ-9 useful
• Baby blues (day 3–10, mild, self-limiting) — safety-net; persistent >2wk = treat as PND
• Safeguarding: consider infant + other children

actions.label: Dental signposting
actions.text:
GPs cannot provide NHS dental treatment and should not routinely prescribe for dental infection — the treatment is dental (drainage/extraction).

Signpost: own dentist (urgent slot) or NHS 111 for urgent dental access if no dentist.

ESCALATE (→ ED): facial swelling spreading to eye or neck, trismus, difficulty swallowing/breathing, systemically unwell (?Ludwig’s angina / spreading odontogenic infection).

rules.label: Results query
patterns: (have|got|chasing|waiting for|when will i get|any news on|where are) (my )?(blood |test )?results

actions.label: Admin routing
actions.text:
Results-status query — admin/clerical, not a clinical queue item.

1. Check if result is filed → if normal & actioned, reception/portal can relay
2. If abnormal/unactioned → route to the requesting clinician
3. If pending → give expected turnaround
4. Signpost NHS App / online record for future self-serve

actions.label: Admin routing
actions.text:
Referral-status query — route to admin team, not the clinical queue.

1. Check referral was sent + e-RS status
2. If 2WW referral and no appointment within expected window → escalate to secretary/2WW chase actively (this one IS time-critical)
3. Routine: signpost NHS App appointment tracking / hospital booking line
4. New symptoms or deterioration while waiting → clinical review, consider expediting letter

rules.label: Letter/report request
patterns: (need|requesting|request|asking for|can i (have|get)) (a )?(letter|medical letter|gp letter|doctor.?s letter|support\w* letter|evidence letter)

actions.label: Admin routing
actions.text:
Letter/report request — admin workflow, not the clinical queue (fit notes have their own rule).

• Non-NHS work: practice may charge (per BMA guidance) — admin to advise fee + timescale before clinician writes
• Factual extracts can often be handled via SAR/online record instead of a bespoke letter
• Route to designated letters clinician/secretary with clear purpose stated

actions.label: Travel health routing
actions.text:
Planned preventive care → practice nurse travel clinic or private travel clinic, ideally 6–8 weeks before departure (some courses need time; late presentation still worth assessing).

• Risk-assess via fitfortravel/NaTHNaC by destination
• Only some vaccines are NHS-funded (typhoid, hep A, cholera, polio-containing booster); yellow fever/rabies/JE = private/registered centre
• Malaria prophylaxis: private prescription
• Returning traveller with FEVER = clinical, not admin (?malaria — same-day)

actions.label: GLP-1 weight-management requests
actions.text:
Rapidly growing request type — route to a planned appointment, not urgent queue.

• NICE: semaglutide (Wegovy, TA875) via specialist weight management; tirzepatide (Mounjaro, TA1026) with phased primary-care rollout from 2025 under strict eligibility (BMI + comorbidity tiers) — CHECK CURRENT LOCAL COMMISSIONING before promising anything
• Ozempic is licensed for T2DM, not weight loss — do not prescribe off-label for weight
• Many patients buy privately online: ask, document, counsel (side effects, gallstones, pancreatitis, pregnancy avoidance)
• Screen for eating disorder before supporting weight-loss medication

actions.label: ACP routing
actions.text:
Advance-care-planning / DNACPR / LPA request — sensitive but planned work: book with the patient’s usual GP (or palliative lead), allow a double slot; not for the urgent queue.

• DNACPR/ReSPECT: clinical decision made WITH patient/family — document and share (incl. out-of-hours/ambulance visibility)
• LPA forms are completed via OPG — GP only needed if asked to act as certificate provider (may carry a fee)
• If the text suggests the patient is actively dying or in symptom crisis → that is urgent clinical work, not admin

actions.label: Memory pathway
actions.text:
Gradual memory decline → routine (not urgent) workup: collateral history, cognitive test (GPCOG/6-CIT), dementia bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, B12/folate, calcium, glucose ± HbA1c), depression screen, medication review (anticholinergic burden) → memory clinic referral.

• ACUTE/fluctuating confusion = delirium → same-day (separate rule)
• Rapid progression over weeks, young onset, neurology → urgent referral
• Driving: advise DVLA notification where diagnosis made

actions.label: How to use
actions.text:
Signpost only — Medicus does not compute these scores.

Open the calculator and enter the inputs from the record:
• QRISK3: age, sex, ethnicity, smoking, systolic BP, total cholesterol:HDL ratio, BMI, comorbidities
• QCancer: age, sex, symptoms, comorbidities
• eFI: coded frailty deficits

Always verify against the patient record.

resultRules.label: Bowel screening: no response
abnormalText: no response to bowel cancer screening
